text: So there's a tumor.
The case begins, in this case, by dividing the posterior pleura.
And in this particular instance, we're going to be doing the dissection from posterior to anterior.
The robot is excellent at dissection of the fissural tissue.
It's very important to remove the lymph nodes.
These are sent for frozen section.
but also in terms of freeing up the tissue to allow passage of instruments to divide the fissure.
So we're going to be using a linear stapler that is completely robotically controlled.
This entire operation is being done by the console surgeon.
No first assistant is really required until it comes time to remove the specimen from the chest.
So the posterior fissure is divided and you'll see that these staplers really give a very even staple line.
Dissection of the posterior ascending artery then is performed.
Again, the lymph node from behind that vessel had been completely removed, and this really allows very safe passage of the robotic staplers.
This artery, if it's small enough, can be tied off or could be even clipped.
However, we're using a vascular load linear stapler on it.
Now at this point, we're going to do further dissection in and around the bronchus.
Again, removing lymphatic tissue allows us to safely navigate around the structure of interest.
There's a little bit of bleeding there from a bronchial vessel.
And now we're going to be using again a robotic stapler, this time using a green load cartridge to divide the bronchus.
So a small flexible suction catheter, which is actually a tracheal suction tube, is used to suction a small amount of blood.
Now once the bronchus has been divided, it can be safely lifted up, elevated off.
This shows nicely the truncus anterior, which likewise is clearative tissue.
And then it's a straight shot from the anterior port to navigate a stapler around that.
And you can use vessel loops if you want to.
If you've done a good enough job removing the nodal tissue, then oftentimes you don't even need a vessel loop to get around the vessels.
So at this point, the lung is retracted posteriorly and the anterior aspect of the hilum is exposed.
Again, the common theme here is removal of nodal tissue to allow dissection of the vessels and bronchi.
In this case, just because the angle is a little bit off, we are using a vessel loop to help tent up the vessel as the linear stapler is applied.
And then lastly, the horizontal fissure between the upper and middle lobes is completed using serial firings of a robotic stapler.
And here, one has to use judgment based on the thickness of tissue, what the best cartridge is, be it blue or green.
In this case, there really was no fissure evident between the upper and middle lobe, so the tissue was rather thick, and so we used a green load.
So now the anterior 12 millimeter port site is extended to approximately four centimeters or so and an impermeable specimen retrieval bag placed.
We use the anchor bag simply because it's made of ripstop nylon and is virtually impossible to perforate.
Chest is irrigated and the bronchial stump is checked, inflating the lung to about 20 centimeters of water.
And then a single 20 or 24 French chest tube is placed again through the anterior port so that it resides in a posterior position.
